items.description: Public struct/type `Greeter` with `greet(name: String) -> String` returning "Hello, <name>!" and `greet() -> String` returning "Hello, world!"

rules_checked.reasoning: Declared kind is 'type' and the item defines a public struct with two methods (greet(name:), greet()); this is a plain value type holding stateless behavior, correctly kinded as a type rather than a protocol, service, or view-model.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift lives in the Greeter target as declared in Package.swift (target(name: "Greeter")), which is the module's only source target and the correct home for its core public type.

Greeter is a plain library type with no view or view-model layer in this package; greet() logic is simple string formatting with no data access, so there is no altitude violation (no domain logic in views, no data access from view models — neither exists here).

The module currently has only GreeterModule.swift, a namespace marker with no behavior. Introducing Greeter as the actual feature type is warranted rather than extending the empty namespace enum, since GreeterModule is explicitly a placeholder ('scaffold seed'), not an existing type with related responsibility to extend.
No protocol is introduced by this item; it's a concrete struct with no abstraction, so A-PROTO's concern about speculative protocols does not apply and is satisfied trivially.

The name 'Greeter' is a noun describing an agent/responsibility (something that greets), matching Swift API Design Guidelines for type naming; method names greet(name:) and greet() are verb-based and read clearly at call sites, consistent with Swift naming conventions.
The type is self-contained within Sources/Greeter with no imports of other modules or forbidden dependencies; it introduces no coupling beyond the target's own module boundary.
